text:
Hybrid porous nanowire arrays, consisting of strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon, were synthesized via the straightforward carbonization of a metal-organic framework (MOF) grown directly on copper foil. This architecture yields a high surface area of $251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$ and a significant carbon content (52.1 wt%), allowing the material to serve as a self-supported binder-free electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). The electrode demonstrates exceptional performance in alkaline media (0.1 and 1.0 M KOH), achieving a low onset potential of 1.47 V vs. RHE and maintaining a stable current density of $10.0\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at 1.52 V for over 30 hours with a Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%. 

This superior catalytic activity and durability, which outperform many state-of-the-art noble-metal and transition-metal catalysts, are attributed to the synergistic effects of the nanowire array configuration and in situ carbon incorporation. These features ensure a large active surface area, rapid mass and charge transport, efficient release of oxygen bubbles, and robust structural integrity. Additionally, the $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$–carbon hybrid exhibits bifunctional activity by effectively gauging the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) through a preferred four-electron pathway. This versatility positions the material as a promising candidate for rechargeable metal-air batteries, regenerative fuel cells, and other advanced clean energy technologies.
